content: The changing seasons on Earth are caused by the way our planet tilts as it orbits the Sun. Imagine Earth as a spinning top that leans slightly to one side—this tilt means that during different parts of the year, either the Northern or Southern Hemisphere leans more toward the Sun. When a hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, it receives more direct sunlight, making it warmer and creating summer. When it tilts away, the sunlight is less direct, so that hemisphere experiences cooler temperatures and winter. This tilt, combined with Earth’s yearly journey around the Sun, creates the familiar cycle of spring, summer, fall, and winter. It’s not about how close we are to the Sun—that distance changes very little—but rather the angle at which sunlight hits the Earth that drives the seasons. This simple tilt shapes the patterns of weather and daylight that affect life and business worldwide.
